title: On Indefinite Integral Ternary Quadratic Forms
speaker: Junho Peter Whang
affiliation: Seoul National University and Princeton University
abstract: We present solutions to two problems on indefinite integral ternary quadratic forms. The first, highlighted by Margulis in 1990, concerns the distribution of the ternary Markoff spectrum associated with minima of forms. The second, initiated by Serre in 1990, is on the asymptotic growth of the number of isotropic forms of bounded height. To overcome difficulties involving high ramification, we develop a tool that proves useful for other distribution problems as well. Joint work with Alexander Gamburd, Amit Ghosh, and Peter Sarnak.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic set-up and Markoff invariant
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes primitive integral nondegenerate indefinite ternary quadratic forms up to integral orthogonal change of variables, introduces determinant, class invariant, and the projective minimum invariant mu, and places these in the ternary Markoff spectrum.

proof_stages.label: Anisotropic Markoff-counting theorem
proof_stages.summary: After recalling Margulis's finiteness question and previous numerical and upper-bound work, the speaker states the main theorem that the multiplicity-counting Markoff counting function grows like gamma x log x, with gamma in principle explicit.

proof_stages.label: Class-number and isotropic-form asymptotics
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states companion results: average class numbers and genera of determinant at most x have x log x asymptotics, weighted isotropic classes have an x/sqrt(log x) asymptotic governed by local densities, and naive counts of isotropic forms in expanding coefficient regions have corresponding X^6/sqrt(log X) asymptotics.

proof_stages.label: Equidistribution bridge from classes to box counts
proof_stages.summary: The isotropic box-counting theorem is reduced to class-sum asymptotics using homogeneous-dynamics equidistribution of representatives from a fixed class in scaled determinant-one regions.

proof_stages.label: Root-packet decomposition by high ramification
proof_stages.summary: Classes are partitioned by root packets, consisting of the local classes at primes where the determinant has high valuation, and the determinant is decomposed into a squarefull ramified part r and an essentially squarefree part s.

proof_stages.label: Packet-wise dominated-convergence strategy
proof_stages.summary: For each main counting problem the normalized contribution is viewed as a function on root packets, and the desired global asymptotic is reduced to proving an integrable uniform majorant and pointwise limits, then summing by dominated convergence.

proof_stages.label: Packet estimates, sieving, and Euler products
proof_stages.summary: The required bounds and limits are established by elementary squarefree-divisor sums for class numbers, Watson-type bounds and sieve amplification for least represented values in the Markoff problem, and a factorized Dirichlet series/Euler-product analysis for isotropic weighted sums.

examples.name: Binary Markoff spectrum and Markoff equation
examples.note: The binary quadratic-form Markoff spectrum, described via positive solutions of the Markoff equation, is used as historical and conceptual contrast for the ternary spectrum rather than as an input to the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: The binary Markoff spectrum and Markoff equation are cited historically as Markoff’s binary analogue, with no calculation or construction carried out on them.

passes.reason: The Markoff equation is a highly permutation-symmetric historical binary analogue, not used to refute a claim.
It is the minimal two-variable Markoff-spectrum case, and the Markoff equation is fully symmetric in its variables, used only as historical comparison.
the Markoff equation is permutation-symmetric in its variables and is cited as the classical binary analogue, not as a refutation

Used as classical background for the ternary spectrum, with the Markoff equation having strong variable symmetries rather than serving as a refutation.
The binary Markoff case is used as a historical low-dimensional analog, with the Markoff equation itself symmetric in its variables.
The binary Markoff spectrum is cited as the classical analogue, and the Markoff equation is structurally fully symmetric in its variables.
It is cited as the historical binary analogue, and the Markoff equation is permutation-symmetric rather than adversarial.
It is used as historical low-dimensional background; the binary case is the minimal indefinite-rank setting and the Markoff equation is highly variable-symmetric.

examples.name: Binary indefinite quadratic-form class numbers
examples.note: The binary case, including Hooley's Pell-equation heuristics and the expected x log^2 x average, illustrates how different the ternary x log x class-number behavior is.
examples.engagement_reason: Binary indefinite quadratic-form class numbers are compared to the ternary case and known/conjectural growth facts are stated, but the speaker does not derive or compute them in the talk.

passes.reason: Binary indefinite-form class numbers are used as the contrasting case where the ternary one-class-per-genus/x log x behavior fails.
The binary case is the minimal-rank indefinite case and is invoked to show that the ternary one-class-per-genus behavior does not generalize.

binary indefinite form class numbers are an ordinary nondegenerate low-dimensional family, but their different growth and non-one-class-per-genus behavior are used to show the ternary phenomenon does not carry over
Binary indefinite class numbers are used as the contrasting case showing the ternary one-class-per-genus behavior does not extend to binary forms.
The binary case is the minimal-dimensional indefinite case and Hooley's result is invoked to show one-class-per-genus behavior fails there.
A standard lower-dimensional comparison family for class-number asymptotics, not deployed as an adversarial example.
Cited to show that the ternary one-class-per-genus/asymptotic behavior does not naively extend to binary indefinite forms.
Used as a contrast showing that the ternary average class-number behavior does not extend to indefinite binary forms.
Binary indefinite class numbers are the minimal-rank analog used to show ternary-style average behavior/one-class-per-genus intuition fails.
Binary indefinite class numbers are used to show the ternary one-class-per-genus or x log x behavior does not extend to dimension two.
It is used as a comparative classical binary analogue for class-number asymptotics, not as a failure case.
Binary indefinite class numbers are invoked to show the analogous ternary one-class-per-genus/x log x behavior fails; binary rank is the minimal indefinite case.

examples.name: Smooth degree-two curves over Q with rational points
examples.note: Counting smooth conics over Q with a rational point is presented as an arithmetic-geometric application of the isotropic ternary-form counting theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker formulates the counting problem for degree-two curves with rational points via isotropic ternary forms and states an asymptotic counting theorem for it.

passes.reason: These are ordinary smooth conics with rational points counted in a density application, not an obstruction or sporadic object.

examples.name: Squarefree determinant forms
examples.note: The squarefree-determinant subcase is cited as a hint for why separating highly ramified primes into root packets should make class and representation counts tractable.
examples.engagement_reason: Squarefree determinant forms are used in the root-packet/asymptotic discussion, including summing over squarefree parts and deriving rough x log x growth behavior.

passes.reason: Squarefree determinant forms are a simplifying unramified subfamily rather than a singular, extremal, or exceptional object.
Squarefree determinant forms have the trivial no-high-ramification root packet, a minimal local-ramification subfamily used for simplification.
squarefree determinant forms are the least-ramified boundary subfamily, essentially the trivial root-packet case, used for simplification rather than contradiction
Squarefree determinant forms lie at the minimal-ramification boundary where determinant valuations are only 0 or 1.
Squarefree determinant forms are the least-ramified/trivial-root-packet boundary subfamily used for comparison, not a counterexample.
Squarefree-determinant forms are a natural infinite subfamily with simple ramification, used for comparison rather than to disprove anything.
Squarefree determinant forms are the least-ramified boundary subfamily in the root-packet decomposition.
Squarefree determinant forms are the minimally ramified boundary subfamily, essentially the trivial root-packet case.
Squarefree determinant forms are the minimal-ramification boundary case, essentially the trivial root-packet subfamily.
Squarefree determinant forms are a minimal-ramification boundary case with no square prime factors in the determinant.
Squarefree determinant forms are the least-ramified or trivial-root-packet subfamily, not a counterexample.
